planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; Florida paspalum is a native perennial bunch grass of moist to dry open habitats throughout the southeastern Coastal Plain, including pine flatwoods, woodland edges, and moist roadsides.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; Florida paspalum is one of the largest-statured native Paspalum species of the southeastern Coastal Plain, forming an erect, robust bunch grass with broad, flat leaves and characteristic two-ranked seed spikes. The plant grows naturally in pine flatwoods, open woodland edges, and moist prairies throughout the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain. As a native grass, it provides structural habitat for grassland birds, the seeds feed numerous ground-foraging birds, and the dense growth supports native insects. In the garden, it functions as a medium-to-large native bunch grass for moist, sunny, naturalistic plantings in zone 9a where it is perennial without irrigation. Good companion for native wildflowers in a longleaf pine ecosystem-style garden design.

common_name: Gulf Muhly (Pink Muhly Grass, Big Muhly)

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in dry, sandy, lean, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Gulf muhly grows naturally in dry, sandy longleaf pine flatwoods and savannas of the Gulf Coastal Plain, where it is an important component of the native bunchgrass community.

directions: Plant in dry, sandy, lean, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Gulf muhly is a larger-statured Muhlenbergia of the longleaf pine savanna ecosystem, producing robust bunchgrass clumps with the same misty, fine-textured pink to purplish seed heads in fall that make Muhlenbergia capillaris (pink muhly) so beloved in ornamental gardens. This species is adapted specifically to the dry, sandy, fire-maintained habitats of the Gulf Coastal Plain longleaf pine ecosystem and is somewhat larger and more heat-tolerant than M. capillaris. The dramatic fall cloud of pink seed heads catches low autumn sun beautifully. In zone 9a, Gulf muhly blooms reliably from September through November, providing late-season interest when most other ornamental grasses have already peaked. Fire-adapted and responds well to early spring burning or cutting back to the ground.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; nimblewill is naturally adapted to shaded conditions and grows in woodland gardens, dry slopes, and disturbed shaded lawns throughout eastern North America.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; nimblewill is a native creeping muhly grass that is one of the more shade-tolerant warm-season grasses of eastern North America, growing at the edges of woodland gardens, on shaded slopes, and in disturbed areas with some shade. The fine-textured, soft green stems root at the nodes and spread to form a low, soft, mat-like ground cover that turns an attractive warm tan-beige in winter. While sometimes treated as a lawn weed, nimblewill is a native grass providing soft ground cover in the dry shade under trees — one of the few warm-season grasses capable of functioning as a lawn alternative in shade. It is the host plant for the Cobweb Skipper butterfly. In winter, the bleached, straw-colored mat persists and actually has decorative winter interest.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in dry, sandy, strongly acidic, infertile soil in full sun; pineywoods dropseed is a native grass of the longleaf pine flatwoods and sandhills ecosystem, adapted to the driest, most infertile sandy soils of the Gulf Coastal Plain.

directions: Plant in dry, sandy, strongly acidic, infertile, well-drained soil in full sun; pineywoods dropseed is a fine-textured, wire-like native bunchgrass of the longleaf pine ecosystem, with very narrow, curling leaves and graceful, open, airy seed heads that appear misty when backlit in fall. It grows in the driest, most infertile, white-sand sites of the Gulf Coastal Plain pine flatwoods — a very specialized habitat. The plant is fire-adapted and is one of the most drought and heat tolerant native grasses of zone 9a. In the garden, it provides a delicate, fine-textured contrast to bold-leaved shade plants and coarser wildflowers in naturalistic pine flatwoods plantings. The seeds are tiny but produced in quantity and consumed by small ground-foraging birds.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (in zone 8–10) or early spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; big quaking grass is a cool-season annual that germinates in fall, overwinters, and sets ornamental seed heads in spring before dying with summer heat.

directions: Direct sow in fall (in the South) or spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; big quaking grass is a cool-season annual from the Mediterranean region grown for its remarkably ornamental, large, heart-shaped, pendant spikelets that tremble and dance in the slightest breeze — earning it the common name and making it one of the most charming ornamental grasses for cut flower arrangements, dried flower crafts, and naturalistic garden borders. The spikelets begin green and ripen through shades of gold and tan. In zone 9a, it thrives as a fall-sown annual that blooms in spring before dying in summer heat. Self-sows to naturalize in appropriate conditions. The dried seed heads retain their ornamental value indefinitely when cut before fully ripe. A beloved cottage garden and cutting garden grass for spring arrangements.
